content:
Hey Ryan,

First, I’d want my child to see that poetry in the Bible tells the truth—just in a different way than a lab report. I’m not going to pit “poetic” against “true.”

What I do next Sunday when Psalm 19 is read with my 10-year-old:
- While it’s read, I’d quietly whisper, “Listen—this is a song. The sky is ‘speaking’ about God without words. Poetry uses pictures to tell real truth.” Then I’d point to “the heavens declare the glory of God.”
- After the service, I’d open the Bible at lunch and read Psalm 19:1–6 again. I’d ask, “Do we say ‘sunrise’ even though the earth is turning?” When they nod, I’d say, “Right. That’s not a lie; it’s how it looks from where we stand. The Bible often speaks like that. Poetry and everyday speech can be perfectly true.”
- Then I’d connect it to Genesis 1: “Genesis is not make-believe. God really created everything by His word. Genesis 1 is written with beauty and pattern, but it’s still telling us what God did. Christians who love the Bible sometimes read the ‘days’ differently, but we all confess: God made all things from nothing, He ordered His world, He made people in His image, and He called it good.”

How I’d pursue unity without surrendering clarity:
- I’d teach my child a simple catechism: Who made you? God. What else did God make? All things. Why did God make you and all things? For His glory. Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 agree on that.
- I’d explain genres simply: “Narrative, poetry, prophecy—different kinds of writing, same truthfulness.” Psalm 19 is poetry; Genesis 1 is majestic, highly patterned prose narrative. Calling something “poetic” doesn’t make it less true, and calling Genesis “poetic” shouldn’t be code for “it didn’t happen.”

What I’d say to my elder (privately, respectfully):
- “I want unity, too. Can you clarify what you mean by ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic’? Do you mean it’s not making claims about what God actually did, or that its exalted style requires care in how we understand ‘day’?”
- “How do you see Exodus 20:11, where the Sabbath command is grounded in God’s six days of work and one of rest? And Jesus and Paul rooting their arguments in creation (Matt 19:4; Rom 5)?”
- “Can we agree to teach our people that genre matters, that inerrancy includes phenomenological and poetic language, and that we mustn’t reduce ‘poetry’ to ‘not history’?”

Bottom line I want my 10-year-old to keep:
- God really made the world.
- The world really tells the truth about Him.
- God’s Word—whether poetry or prose—never lies.
- We show kindness to Christians who differ on the length of the days while we hold fast to what Scripture clearly teaches.

I’d put an arm around my kid during Psalm 19 and let them feel the wonder: the sky is preaching without words, and the same God who painted that sky spoke the world into being.
